kill_chain_phase.name: kill_chain_phase
kill_chain_phase.description: The Kill Chain Phase object represents a single phase of a cyber attack, including the initial reconnaissance and planning stages up to the final objective of the attacker. It provides a detailed description of each phase and its associated activities within the broader context of a cyber attack. See Cyber Kill Chain®.
kill_chain_phase.caption: Kill Chain Phase

name.description: The operating system name.
name.caption: Name

type.description: The type of the operating system.
type.caption: Type

version.description: The version of the OS running on the device that originated the event. For example: "Windows 10", "OS X 10.7", or "iOS 9".
version.caption: Version

country.description:
The operating system country code, as defined by the ISO 3166-1 standard (Alpha-2 code).

Note: The two letter country code should be capitalized. For example: US or CA.
country.caption: Country

lang.description: The two letter lower case language codes, as defined by ISO 639-1. For example: en (English), de (German), or fr (French).
lang.caption: Language

99.description: The type is not mapped. See the type attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

type_id.description: The type identifier of the operating system.
type_id.caption: Type ID

cpe_name.description: The Common Platform Enumeration (CPE) name as described by (NIST) For example: cpe:/a:apple:safari:16.2.
cpe_name.caption: The product CPE identifier

cpu_bits.description: The cpu architecture, the number of bits used for addressing in memory. For example: 32 or 64.
cpu_bits.caption: CPU Bits

edition.description: The operating system edition. For example: Professional.
edition.caption: OS Edition

kernel_release.description: The kernel release of the operating system. On Unix-based systems, this is determined from the uname -r command output, for example "5.15.0-122-generic".
kernel_release.caption: Kernel Release

sp_name.description: The name of the latest Service Pack.
sp_name.caption: OS Service Pack

sp_ver.description: The version number of the latest Service Pack.
sp_ver.caption: OS Service Pack Version

os.name: os
os.description: The Operating System (OS) object describes characteristics of an OS, such as Linux or Windows.
os.caption: Operating System (OS)

control.description: A Control is a prescriptive, actionable set of specifications that strengthens device posture. The control specifies required security measures, while the specific implementation values are defined in control_parameters. E.g., CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark 1.2.0 - Control 2.1 - Ensure CloudTrail is enabled in all regions
control.caption: Security Control

status.description: The resultant status of the compliance check normalized to the caption of the status_id value. In the case of 'Other', it is defined by the event source.
status.caption: Status

desc.description: The description or criteria of a control.
desc.caption: Description

category.description: The category a control framework pertains to, as reported by the source tool, such as Asset Management or Risk Assessment.
category.caption: Category

assessments.description: A list of assessments associated with the compliance requirements evaluation.
assessments.caption: Assessments

checks.description: A list of compliance checks associated with specific industry standards or frameworks. Each check represents an individual rule or requirement that has been evaluated against a target device. Checks typically include details such as the check name (e.g., CIS: 'Ensure mounting of cramfs filesystems is disabled' or DISA STIG descriptive titles), unique identifiers (such as CIS identifier '1.1.1.1' or DISA STIG identifier 'V-230234'), descriptions (detailed explanations of security requirements or vulnerability discussions), and version information.
checks.caption: Compliance Checks

compliance_references.description: A list of reference KB articles that provide information to help organizations understand, interpret, and implement compliance standards. They provide guidance, best practices, and examples.
compliance_references.caption: Compliance Standard References

@deprecated.message: Use the Compliance object with Check array instead.

compliance_standards.description: A list of established guidelines or criteria that define specific requirements an organization must follow.
compliance_standards.caption: Compliance Standards: Details

@deprecated.message: Use the Compliance object with Check array instead.

control_parameters.description: The list of control parameters evaluated in a Compliance check. E.g., parameters for CloudTrail configuration might include multiRegionTrailEnabled: true, logFileValidationEnabled: true, and requiredRegions: [us-east-1, us-west-2]
control_parameters.caption: Control Parameters

requirements.description: The specific compliance requirements being evaluated. E.g., PCI DSS Requirement 8.2.3 - Passwords must meet minimum complexity requirements or HIPAA Security Rule 164.312(a)(2)(iv) - Implement encryption and decryption mechanisms
requirements.caption: Compliance Requirements

standards.description: The regulatory or industry standards being evaluated for compliance.
standards.caption: Compliance Standards: List

status_code.description: The resultant status code of the compliance check.
status_code.caption: Status Code

status_detail.description: The contextual description of the status, status_code values.
status_detail.caption: Status Detail

status_details.description: A list of contextual descriptions of the status, status_code values.
status_details.caption: Status Details

3.description: The compliance check failed for at least one of the evaluated resources.
3.caption: Fail

1.description: The compliance check passed for all the evaluated resources.
1.caption: Pass

2.description: The compliance check did not yield a result due to missing information.
2.caption: Warning

99.description: The status is not mapped. See the status attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

status_id.description: The normalized status identifier of the compliance check.
status_id.caption: Status ID

compliance.name: compliance
compliance.description: The Compliance object contains information about Industry and Regulatory Framework standards, controls and requirements or details about custom assessments utilized in a compliance evaluation. Standards define broad security frameworks, controls represent specific security requirements within those frameworks, and checks are the testable verification points used to determine if controls are properly implemented.
compliance.caption: Compliance

version.description: The D3FEND™ Matrix version.
version.caption: Version

d3f_tactic.description: The Tactic object describes the tactic ID and/or name that is associated with a countermeasure.
d3f_tactic.caption: MITRE D3FEND™ Tactic

d3f_technique.description: The Technique object describes the technique ID and/or name associated with a countermeasure.
d3f_technique.caption: MITRE D3FEND™ Technique

d3fend.name: d3fend
d3fend.description: The MITRE D3FEND™ object describes the tactic & technique associated with a countermeasure.
d3fend.caption: MITRE D3FEND™

size.description: The size in bytes of the email, including attachments.
size.caption: Size

from_list.description: The machine-readable email header From values. This array should contain the value in from. For example example.user@usersdomain.com.
from_list.caption: From List

uid.description: The unique identifier of the email thread.
uid.caption: Email Thread UID

files.description: The files embedded or attached to the email.
files.caption: Files

from.description: The machine-readable email header From value, as defined by RFC 5322. For example example.user@usersdomain.com.
from.caption: From

cc.description: The machine-readable email header Cc values, as defined by RFC 5322. For example example.user@usersdomain.com.
cc.caption: Cc

to.description: The machine-readable email header To values, as defined by RFC 5322. For example example.user@usersdomain.com
to.caption: To

sender.description: The machine readable email address of the system or server that actually transmitted the email message, extracted from the email headers per RFC 5322. This differs from the from field, which shows the message author. The sender field is most commonly used when multiple addresses appear in the from_list field, or when the transmitting system is different from the message author (such as when sending on behalf of someone else).
sender.caption: Sender

subject.description: The email header Subject value, as defined by RFC 5322.
subject.caption: Subject

cc_mailboxes.description: The human-readable email header Cc Mailbox values. For example 'Example User <example.user@usersdomain.com>'.
cc_mailboxes.caption: Cc Mailboxes

data_classification.description: The Data Classification object includes information about data classification levels and data category types.
data_classification.caption: Data Classification

data_classifications.description: A list of Data Classification objects, that include information about data classification levels and data category types, identified by a classifier.
data_classifications.caption: Data Classification

delivered_to.description: The machine-readable Delivered-To email header field. For example example.user@usersdomain.com
delivered_to.caption: Delivered To

delivered_to_list.description: The machine-readable Delivered-To email header values. For example example.user@usersdomain.com
delivered_to_list.caption: Delivered To List

from_mailbox.description: The human-readable email header From Mailbox value. For example 'Example User <example.user@usersdomain.com>'.
from_mailbox.caption: From Mailbox

from_mailboxes.description: The human-readable email header From Mailbox values. This array should contain the value in from_mailbox. For example 'Example User <example.user@usersdomain.com>'.
from_mailboxes.caption: From Mailboxes

http_headers.description: Additional HTTP headers of an HTTP request or response.
http_headers.caption: HTTP Headers

is_read.description: The indication of whether the email has been read.
is_read.caption: Read

message_uid.description: The email header Message-ID value, as defined by RFC 5322.
message_uid.caption: Message UID

reply_to.description: The machine-readable email header Reply-To value, as defined by RFC 5322. For example example.user@usersdomain.com
reply_to.caption: Reply To

reply_to_list.description: The machine-readable email header Reply-To values, as defined by RFC 5322. For example example.user@usersdomain.com
reply_to_list.caption: Reply To List

reply_to_mailboxes.description: The human-readable email header Reply To Mailbox values. For example 'Example User <example.user@usersdomain.com>'.
reply_to_mailboxes.caption: Reply To Mailboxes

return_path.description: The address found in the 'Return-Path' header, which indicates where bounce messages (non-delivery reports) should be sent. This address is often set by the sending system and may differ from the 'From' or 'Sender' addresses. For example, mailer-daemon@senderserver.com.
return_path.caption: Return Path

sender_mailbox.description: The human readable email address of the system or server that actually transmitted the email message, extracted from the email headers per RFC 5322. This differs from the from_mailbox field, which shows the message author. The sender mailbox field is most commonly used when multiple addresses appear in the from_mailboxes field, or when the transmitting system is different from the message author (such as when sending on behalf of someone else).
sender_mailbox.caption: Sender Mailbox

smtp_from.description: The value of the SMTP MAIL FROM command.
smtp_from.caption: SMTP From

smtp_to.description: The value of the SMTP envelope RCPT TO command.
smtp_to.caption: SMTP To

to_mailboxes.description: The human-readable email header To Mailbox values. For example 'Example User <example.user@usersdomain.com>'.
to_mailboxes.caption: To Mailboxes

urls.description: The URLs embedded in the email.
urls.caption: URLs

x_originating_ip.description: The X-Originating-IP header identifying the emails originating IP address(es).
x_originating_ip.caption: X-Originating-IP

email.name: email
email.description: The Email object describes the email metadata such as sender, recipients, and direction, and can include embedded URLs and files.
email.caption: Email

name.description: The name of the rule that generated the event.
name.caption: Name

type.description: The rule type.
type.caption: Type

version.description: The rule version. For example: 1.1.
version.caption: Version

desc.description: The description of the rule that generated the event.
desc.caption: Description

uid.description: The unique identifier of the rule that generated the event.
uid.caption: Unique ID

duration.description: The rule response time duration, usually used for challenge completion time.
duration.caption: Duration Milliseconds

condition.description: The rule trigger condition for the rule. For example: SQL_INJECTION.
condition.caption: Condition

match_details.description: The data in a request that rule matched. For example: '["10","and","1"]'.
match_details.caption: Match Details

match_location.description: The location of the matched data in the source which resulted in the triggered firewall rule. For example: HEADER.
match_location.caption: Match Location

rate_limit.description: The rate limit for a rate-based rule.
rate_limit.caption: Rate Limit

sensitivity.description: The sensitivity of the firewall rule in the matched event. For example: HIGH.
sensitivity.caption: Sensitivity

firewall_rule.name: firewall_rule
firewall_rule.description: The Firewall Rule object represents a specific rule within a firewall policy or event. It contains information about a rule's configuration, properties, and associated actions that define how network traffic is handled by the firewall.
firewall_rule.caption: Firewall Rule

driver.description: The startup item kernel driver resource.
driver.caption: Kernel Driver

name.description: The unique name of the startup item.
name.caption: Name

type.description: The startup item type.
type.caption: Type

start_type.description: The start type of the startup item.
start_type.caption: Start Type

3.description: A background process typically managed by the operating system, e.g., a service process on Windows or a systemd-managed daemon on Linux.
3.caption: Service

6.description: System extensions on macOS enables 3rd parties to extend the capabilities of macOS.
6.caption: System Extension

1.description: Kernel mode driver.
1.caption: Kernel Mode Driver

2.description: User mode driver.
2.caption: User Mode Driver

99.description: The startup item type is not mapped. See the type attribute, which contains data source specific values.
99.caption: Other

4.description: An application that runs in the user space.
4.caption: User Mode Application

5.description: The macOS Autoload Application.
5.caption: Autoload

7.description: Kernel extensions on macOS includes Apple provided pre-installs and 3rd party installs which enables support for specific hardware or software features not natively supported by macOS.
7.caption: Kernel Extension

8.description: A job or task that runs on a configured schedule.
8.caption: Scheduled Job, Task

type_id.description: The startup item type identifier.
type_id.caption: Type ID

3.description: The startup item runs in a shared process.
3.caption: Shared Process

1.description: The startup item interacts with the desktop.
1.caption: Interactive

2.description: The startup item runs in its own process.
2.caption: Own Process

99.description: The run mode is not mapped. See the run_modes attribute, which contains data source specific values.
99.caption: Other

run_mode_ids.description: The list of normalized identifiers that describe the startup items' properties when it is running.  Use this field to capture extended information about the process, which may depend on the type of startup item.  E.g., A Windows service that interacts with the desktop.
run_mode_ids.caption: Run Mode IDs

run_modes.description: The list of run_modes, normalized to the captions of the run_mode_id values.  In the case of 'Other', they are defined by the event source.
run_modes.caption: Run Modes

run_state.description: The run state of the startup item.
run_state.caption: Run State

3.description: The service is stopping.
3.caption: Stop Pending

6.description: The service is pending pause.
6.caption: Pause Pending

1.description: The service is not running.
1.caption: Stopped

2.description: The service is starting.
2.caption: Start Pending

99.description: The run state is not mapped. See the run_state attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

4.description: The service is running.
4.caption: Running

5.description: The service is pending continue.
5.caption: Continue Pending

7.description: The service is paused.
7.caption: Paused

8.description: The service is pending restart.
8.caption: Restart Pending

run_state_id.description: The run state ID of the startup item.
run_state_id.caption: Run State ID

3.description: Started on demand. For example, by the Windows Service Control Manager when a process calls the StartService function.
3.caption: On Demand

6.description: Started on specific user logins.
6.caption: Specific User Login

1.description: Service started automatically during system startup.
1.caption: Auto

2.description: Device driver started by the system loader.
2.caption: Boot

99.description: The start type is not mapped. See the start_type attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

4.description: The service is disabled, and cannot be started.
4.caption: Disabled

5.description: Started on all user logins.
5.caption: All Logins

7.description: Stared according to a schedule.
7.caption: Scheduled

8.description: Started when a system item, such as a file or registry key, changes.
8.caption: System Changed

start_type_id.description: The start type ID of the startup item.
start_type_id.caption: Start Type ID

win_service.description: The startup item Windows service resource.
win_service.caption: Windows Service

startup_item.name: startup_item
startup_item.description: The startup item object describes an application component that has associated startup criteria and configurations.
startup_item.caption: Startup Item

command_response.description: The reply to the request command (e.g. RESPONSE, BINDACK or FAULT).
command_response.caption: Command Response

opnum.description: An operation number used to identify a specific remote procedure call (RPC) method or a method in an interface.
opnum.caption: Opnum

rpc_interface.description: The RPC Interface object describes the details pertaining to the remote procedure call interface.
rpc_interface.caption: Remote Procedure Call Interface

dce_rpc.name: dce_rpc
dce_rpc.description: The DCE/RPC, or Distributed Computing Environment/Remote Procedure Call, object describes the remote procedure call system for distributed computing environments.
dce_rpc.caption: DCE/RPC

data.description: Additional data about the node stored as key-value pairs. Can include custom properties specific to the node.
data.caption: Data

name.description: A human-readable name or label for the node. Should be descriptive and unique within the graph context.
name.caption: Name

type.description: Categorizes the node into a specific class or type. Useful for grouping and filtering nodes.
type.caption: Type

desc.description: A human-readable description of the node's purpose or meaning in the graph.
desc.caption: Description

uid.description: A unique string or numeric identifier that distinguishes this node from all others in the graph. Must be unique across all nodes.
uid.caption: Unique ID

node.name: node
node.description: Represents a node or a vertex in a graph structure.
node.caption: Node

name.description: The HTTP cookie name.
name.caption: Name

path.description: The path of the HTTP cookie.
path.caption: Path

domain.description: The domain name for the server from which the http_cookie is served.
domain.caption: Domain

secure.description: The cookie attribute to only send cookies to the server with an encrypted request over the HTTPS protocol.
secure.caption: Secure

http_only.description: A cookie attribute to make it inaccessible via JavaScript
http_only.caption: HTTP Only

expiration_time.description: The expiration time of the HTTP cookie.
expiration_time.caption: Expiration Time

is_http_only.description: This attribute prevents the cookie from being accessed via JavaScript.
is_http_only.caption: HTTP Only

is_secure.description: The cookie attribute indicates that cookies are sent to the server only when the request is encrypted using the HTTPS protocol.
is_secure.caption: Secure

samesite.description: The cookie attribute that lets servers specify whether/when cookies are sent with cross-site requests. Values are: Strict, Lax or None
samesite.caption: SameSite

expiration_time_dt.description: The expiration time of the HTTP cookie.
expiration_time_dt.caption: Expiration Time

http_cookie.name: http_cookie
http_cookie.description: The HTTP Cookie object, also known as a web cookie or browser cookie, contains details and values pertaining to a small piece of data that a server sends to a user's web browser. This data is then stored by the browser and sent back to the server with subsequent requests, allowing the server to remember and track certain information about the user's browsing session or preferences.
http_cookie.caption: HTTP Cookie

name.description: The malware name, as reported by the detection engine.
name.caption: Name

path.description: The filesystem path of the malware that was observed.
path.caption: Path

uid.description: A unique identifier for the specific malware instance, as assigned by the detection engine (e.g., virus signature ID or IPS rule ID).
uid.caption: Unique ID

files.description: The list of file objects representing files that were identified as infected by the malware.
files.caption: Files

severity.description: The severity of the malware, normalized to the captions of the severity_id values. In the case of 'Other', they are defined by the event source.
severity.caption: Severity

99.description: The classification is not mapped. See the classifications attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

classification_ids.description: The list of normalized identifiers of the malware classifications.
classification_ids.caption: Classification IDs

classifications.description: The list of malware classifications, normalized to the captions of the classification_ids values. In the case of 'Other', they are defined by the event source.
classifications.caption: Classifications

cves.description: The list of Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) identifiers associated with the malware. Reference: CVE
cves.caption: CVE List

num_infected.description: The number of files that were identified to be infected by the malware.
num_infected.caption: Number of Infected Entities

provider.description: The name or identifier of the security solution or service that provided the malware detection information.
provider.caption: Provider

3.description: Action is required but the situation is not serious at this time.
3.caption: Medium

6.description: An error occurred but it is too late to take remedial action.
6.caption: Fatal

1.description: Informational message. No action required.
1.caption: Informational

2.description: The user decides if action is needed.
2.caption: Low

99.description: The event/finding severity is not mapped. See the severity attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

4.description: Action is required immediately.
4.caption: High

5.description: Action is required immediately and the scope is broad.
5.caption: Critical

severity_id.description: The normalized identifier of the malware severity.
severity_id.caption: Severity ID

malware.name: malware
malware.description: The Malware object describes the classification of known malicious software, which is intentionally designed to cause damage to a computer, server, client, or computer network.
malware.caption: Malware

end_line.description: The line number of the last line of the file, where the information was discovered.
end_line.caption: End Line

cell_name.description: The cell name/reference in a spreadsheet. e.g A2
cell_name.caption: Cell Name

column_name.description: The column name in a spreadsheet, where the information was discovered.
column_name.caption: Column Name

column_number.description: The column number in a spreadsheet or a plain text document, where the information was discovered.
column_number.caption: Column Number

json_path.description: The JSON path of the attribute in a json record, where the information was discovered
json_path.caption: JSON Path

page_number.description: The page number in a document, where the information was discovered.
page_number.caption: Page Number

record_index_in_array.description: The index of the record in the array of records, where the information was discovered. e.g. the index of a record in an array of JSON records in a file.
record_index_in_array.caption: Record Index in Array

row_number.description: The row number in a spreadsheet, where the information was discovered.
row_number.caption: Row Number

start_line.description: The line number of the first line of the file, where the information was discovered.
start_line.caption: Start Line

occurrence_details.name: occurrence_details
occurrence_details.description: Details about where in the target entity, specified information was discovered. Only the attributes, relevant to the target entity type should be populated.
occurrence_details.caption: Occurrence Details

name.description: Unique identifier for the script or macro, independent of the containing file, used for tracking, auditing, and security analysis.
name.caption: Name

type.description: The script type, normalized to the caption of the type_id value. In the case of 'Other', it is defined by the event source.
type.caption: Type

file.description: Present if this script is associated with a file. Not present in the case of a file-less script.
file.caption: File

uid.description: Some script engines assign a unique ID to each individual execution of a given script. This attribute captures that unique ID. In the case of PowerShell, the unique ID corresponds to the ScriptBlockId in the raw ETW events provided by the OS.
uid.caption: Unique ID

99.description: The script type is not mapped. See the type attribute which contains an event source specific value.
99.caption: Other

type_id.description: The normalized script type ID.
type_id.caption: Type ID

hashes.description: An array of the script's cryptographic hashes. Note that these hashes are calculated on the script in its original encoding, and not on the normalized UTF-8 encoding found in the script_content attribute.
hashes.caption: Hashes

parent_uid.description: This attribute relates a sub-script to a parent script having the matching uid attribute. In the case of PowerShell, sub-script execution can be identified by matching the activity correlation ID of the raw ETW events provided by the OS.
parent_uid.caption: Parent Unique ID

script_content.description: The script content, normalized to UTF-8 encoding irrespective of its original encoding. When emitting this attribute, it may be appropriate to truncate large scripts. When consuming this attribute, large scripts should be anticipated.
script_content.caption: Script Content

script.name: script
script.description: The Script object describes a script or command that can be executed by a shell, script engine, or interpreter. Examples include Bash, JavsScript, PowerShell, Python, VBScript, etc. Note that the term script here denotes not only a script contained within a file but also a script or command typed interactively by a user, supplied on the command line, or provided by some other file-less mechanism.
script.caption: Script

name.description: The name of the privilege, action, or permission. Examples: GetObject, CreateStoreImageTask (AWS); Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/read (Azure); storage.objects.get (GCP).
name.caption: Name

type.description: The type or category of the privilege, normalized to the caption of the type_id value. In the case of 'Other', it is defined by the event source.
type.caption: Type

is_unused.description: Indicates whether the privilege is unused within the analysis timeframe.
is_unused.caption: Is Unused

3.description: An execute-type privilege that permits running operations or invoking functions. Examples: lambda:InvokeFunction, Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action.
3.caption: Execute

1.description: A read-type privilege that permits viewing or retrieving data. Examples: s3:GetObject, Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/read, storage.objects.get.
1.caption: Read

2.description: A write-type privilege that permits creating, modifying, or deleting data. Examples: s3:PutObject, Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/write, storage.objects.create.
2.caption: Write

99.description: The privilege type is not mapped. See the type attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

type_id.description: The normalized type of the privilege.
type_id.caption: Type ID

last_used_time.description: The most recent time this privilege was used.
last_used_time.caption: Last Used Time

last_used_time_dt.description: The most recent time this privilege was used.
last_used_time_dt.caption: Last Used Time

privilege_info.name: privilege_info
privilege_info.description: The Privilege Info object describes information about a specific privilege, action, or permission. It captures the privilege name, type, usage status, and when it was last used.
privilege_info.caption: Privilege Info

code.description: The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) status code returned from the web server to the client. For example, 200.
code.caption: Response Code

message.description: The description of the event/finding, as defined by the source.
message.caption: Message

status.description: The response status. For example: A successful HTTP status of 'OK' which corresponds to a code of 200.
status.caption: Status

length.description: The length of the entire HTTP response, in number of bytes.
length.caption: Response Length

content_type.description: The request header that identifies the original media type of the resource (prior to any content encoding applied for sending).
content_type.caption: HTTP Content Type

body_length.description: The actual length of the HTTP response body, in number of bytes, independent of a potentially existing Content-Length header.
body_length.caption: Response Body Length

http_headers.description: Additional HTTP headers of an HTTP request or response.
http_headers.caption: HTTP Headers

latency.description: The HTTP response latency measured in milliseconds.
latency.caption: Latency

http_response.name: http_response
http_response.description: The HTTP Response object contains detailed information about the response sent from a web server to the requester. It encompasses attributes and metadata that describe the response status, headers, body content, and other relevant information.
http_response.caption: HTTP Response

status.description: The resultant status of the classification job normalized to the caption of the status_id value. In the case of 'Other', it is defined by the event source.
status.caption: Status

total.description: The total count of discovered entities, by the classification job.
total.caption: Total

uid.description: The unique identifier of the classification job.
uid.caption: Unique ID

category.description: The name of the data classification category that data matched into, e.g. Financial, Personal, Governmental, etc.
category.caption: Category

pattern_match.description: A text, binary, file name, or datastore that matched against a detection rule.
pattern_match.caption: Pattern Match

3.description: Any financially-related sensitive information or Cardholder Data (CHD). E.g., banking account numbers, credit card numbers, International Banking Account Numbers (IBAN), SWIFT codes, etc.
3.caption: Financial

6.description: Any sensitive security-related data such as passwords, passkeys, IP addresses, API keys, credentials and similar secrets. E.g., AWS Access Secret Key, SaaS API Keys, user passwords, database credentials, etc.
6.caption: Security

0.description: The type is not mapped. See the data_type attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
0.caption: Unknown

1.description: Any Personally Identifiable Information (PII), Electronic Personal Health Information (ePHI), or similarly personal information. E.g., full name, home address, date of birth, etc.
1.caption: Personal

2.description: Any sensitive government identification number related to a person or other classified material. E.g., Passport numbers, driver license numbers, business identification, taxation identifiers, etc.
2.caption: Governmental

99.description: Any other type of data classification or a multi-variate classification made up of several other classification categories.
99.caption: Other

4.description: Any business-specific sensitive data such as intellectual property, trademarks, copyrights, human resource data, Board of Directors meeting minutes, and similar.
4.caption: Business

5.description: Any mission-specific sensitive data for military, law enforcement, or other government agencies such as specifically classified data, weapon systems information, or other planning data.
5.caption: Military and Law Enforcement

category_id.description: The normalized identifier of the data classification category.
category_id.caption: Category ID

classifier_details.description: Describes details about the classifier used for data classification.
classifier_details.caption: Classifier Details

confidentiality.description: The file content confidentiality, normalized to the confidentiality_id value. In the case of 'Other', it is defined by the event source.
confidentiality.caption: Confidentiality

99.description: The confidentiality is not mapped. See the confidentiality attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

confidentiality_id.description: The normalized identifier of the file content confidentiality indicator.
confidentiality_id.caption: Confidentiality ID

data_lifecycle_state.description: The name of the stage or state that the data was in. E.g., Data-at-Rest, Data-in-Transit, etc.
data_lifecycle_state.caption: Data Lifecycle State

3.description: The data was being processed, accessed, or read by a system, making it active in memory or CPU. E.g., sensitive data in a Business Intelligence tool, ePHI being processed in an EHR application or a user viewing data stored in a spreadsheet or PDF.
3.caption: Data in-Use

0.description: The data lifecycle state is unknown.
0.caption: Unknown

1.description: The data was stored on physical or logical media and was not actively moving through the network nor was being processed. E.g., data stored in a database, PDF files in a file share, or EHR records in object storage.
1.caption: Data at-Rest

2.description: The data was actively moving through the network or from one physical or logical location to another. E.g., emails being send, data replication or Change Data Capture (CDC) streams, or sensitive data processed on an API.
2.caption: Data in-Transit

99.description: The data lifecycle state is not mapped. See the data_lifecycle_state attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

data_lifecycle_state_id.description: The stage or state that the data was in when it was assessed or scanned by a data security tool.
data_lifecycle_state_id.caption: Data Lifecycle State ID

detection_pattern.description: Specific pattern, algorithm, fingerprint, or model used for detection.
detection_pattern.caption: Detection Pattern

detection_system.description: The name of the type of data security tool or system that the finding, detection, or alert originated from. E.g., Endpoint, Secure Email Gateway, etc.
detection_system.caption: Detection System

3.description: A Mobile Device Management (MDM) or Enterprise Mobility Management (EMM) tool that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies on mobile devices (e.g., cellphones, tablets, End User Devices [EUDs]).
3.caption: Mobile Device Management

6.description: A Secure Email Gateway (SEG) is any tool that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies within email systems. E.g., Microsoft Defender for Office, Google Workspaces.
6.caption: Secure Email Gateway

0.description: The type is not mapped. See the detection_system attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
0.caption: Unknown

1.description: A dedicated agent or sensor installed on a device, either a dedicated data security tool or an Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) tool that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies. E.g., Forcepoint DLP, Symantec DLP, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (MDE).
1.caption: Endpoint

2.description: A Data Loss Prevention (DLP) gateway that is positioned in-line of an information store such as a network share, a database, or otherwise that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies.
2.caption: DLP Gateway

99.description: Any other type of detection system or a multi-variate system made up of several other systems.
99.caption: Other

4.description: A tool that actively identifies and classifies sensitive data in digital media and information stores in accordance with a policy or automated functionality. E.g, Amazon Macie, Microsoft Purview.
4.caption: Data Discovery & Classification

5.description: A Secure Web Gateway (SWG) is any tool that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies at a network-edge such as within a proxy or firewall service.
5.caption: Secure Web Gateway

7.description: A Digital Rights Management (DRM) or a dedicated Information Rights Management (IRM) are tools which can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies on digital media via policy or user access rights.
7.caption: Digital Rights Management

8.description: A Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies in-line to cloud systems such as the public cloud or Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) tool. E.g., Forcepoint CASB, SkyHigh Security.
8.caption: Cloud Access Security Broker

9.description: A Database Activity Monitoring (DAM) tool that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies as part of a dedicated database or warehouse monitoring solution.
9.caption: Database Activity Monitoring

10.description: A built in Data Loss Prevention (DLP) or other data security capability within a tool or platform such as an Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) or Customer Relations Management (CRM) tool that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies.
10.caption: Application-Level DLP

11.description: Any Developer Security tool such as an Infrastructure-as-Code (IAC) security scanner, Secrets Detection, or Secure Software Development Lifecycle (SSDLC) tool that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies. E.g., TruffleHog, GitGuardian, Git-Secrets.
11.caption: Developer Security

12.description: A Data Security Posture Management (DSPM) tool is a continuous monitoring and data discovery solution that can detect sensitive data and/or enforce data security policies for local and cloud environments. E.g., Cyera, Sentra, IBM Polar Security.
12.caption: Data Security Posture Management

detection_system_id.description: The type of data security tool or system that the finding, detection, or alert originated from.

status_details.description: The contextual description of the status, status_id value.

status_id.caption: Status ID

data_security.description: The Data Security object describes the characteristics, techniques and content of a Data Loss Prevention (DLP), Data Loss Detection (DLD), Data Classification, or similar tools' finding, alert, or detection mechanism(s).
data_security.caption: Data Security

type.description: The type of time span duration the object represents.

start_time.description: The start time or beginning of the timespan's interval.

9.description: The start_time and end_time should be set.

end_time.description: The end time or conclusion of the timespan's interval.

start_time_dt.description: The start time or beginning of the timespan's interval.

end_time_dt.description: The end time or conclusion of the timespan's interval.
end_time_dt.caption: End Time

duration_hours.description: The duration of the time span in hours.
duration_hours.caption: Duration Hours

duration_months.description: The duration of the time span in months.

duration_weeks.description: The duration of the time span in weeks.

duration_years.description: The duration of the time span in years.

timespan.name: timespan
timespan.description:
The Time Span object represents different time period durations. If a timespan is fractional, i.e. crosses one period, e.g. a week and 3 days, more than one may be populated since each member is of integral type. In that case type_id if present should be set to Other.

A timespan may also be defined by its time interval boundaries, start_time and end_time.
timespan.caption: Time Span

name.description: The username. For example, janedoe1.
name.caption: Name

type.description: The type of the user. For example, System, AWS IAM User, etc.
type.caption: Type

domain.description: The domain where the user is defined. For example: the LDAP or Active Directory domain.
domain.caption: Domain

uid.description: The unique user identifier. For example, the Windows user SID, ActiveDirectory DN or AWS user ARN.
uid.caption: Unique ID

groups.description: The administrative groups to which the user belongs.
groups.caption: Groups

org.description: Organization and org unit related to the user.
org.caption: Organization

full_name.description: The full name of the user, as reported by the product.
full_name.caption: Full Name

3.description: System account. For example, Windows computer accounts with a trailing dollar sign ($).
3.caption: System

1.description: Regular user account.
1.caption: User

2.description: Admin/root user account.
2.caption: Admin

99.description: The type is not mapped. See the type attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

4.description: Service account. For example, Windows service account.
4.caption: Service

type_id.description: The account type identifier.
type_id.caption: Type ID

account.description: The user's account or the account associated with the user.
account.caption: Account

credential_uid.description: The unique identifier of the user's credential. For example, AWS Access Key ID.

display_name.description: The display name of the user, as reported by the product.

ldap_person.description: The additional LDAP attributes that describe a person.

phone_number.description: The telephone number of the user.

programmatic_credentials.description: Details about the programmatic credential (API keys, access tokens, certificates, etc) associated to the user.
programmatic_credentials.caption: Programmatic Credentials

risk_level.description: The risk level, normalized to the caption of the risk_level_id value.
risk_level.caption: Risk Level

99.description: The risk level is not mapped. See the risk_level attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

risk_score.description: The risk score as reported by the event source.
risk_score.caption: Risk Score

uid_alt.description: The alternate user identifier. For example, the Active Directory user GUID or AWS user Principal ID.
uid_alt.caption: Alternate ID

user.name: user
user.description: The User object describes the characteristics of a user/person or a security principal.
user.caption: User

observation_parameter.description: The specific parameter or property being monitored. Examples include: CPU usage percentage, API response time in milliseconds, HTTP error rate, memory utilization, network latency, transaction volume, etc.
observation_parameter.caption: Observation Parameter

observation_type.description: The type of analysis being performed to establish baseline behavior. Common types include: Frequency Analysis, Time Pattern Analysis, Volume Analysis, Sequence Analysis, Distribution Analysis, etc.
observation_type.caption: Observation Type

observations.description: Collection of actual measured values, data points and observations recorded for this baseline.
observations.caption: Observations

observed_pattern.description: The specific pattern identified within the observation type. For Frequency Analysis, this could be 'FREQUENT', 'INFREQUENT', 'RARE', or 'UNSEEN'. For Time Pattern Analysis, this could be 'BUSINESS_HOURS', 'OFF_HOURS', or 'UNUSUAL_TIME'. For Volume Analysis, this could be 'NORMAL_VOLUME', 'HIGH_VOLUME', or 'SURGE'. The pattern values are specific to each observation type and indicate the baseline behavior.
observed_pattern.caption: Observed Pattern

baseline.name: baseline
baseline.description: Describes the baseline or expected behavior of a system, service, or component based on historical observations and measurements. It establishes reference points for comparison to detect anomalies, trends, and deviations from typical patterns.
baseline.caption: Baseline

name.description: The name of the SSO resource.
name.caption: Name

uid.description: A unique identifier for a SSO resource.
uid.caption: Unique ID

protocol_name.description: The supported protocol for the SSO resource. E.g., SAML or OIDC.
protocol_name.caption: Supported Protocol

certificate.description: Digital Signature associated with the SSO resource, e.g., SAML X.509 certificate details.
certificate.caption: SAML Certificate

idle_timeout.description: Duration (in minutes) of allowed inactivity before Single Sign-On (SSO) session expiration.
idle_timeout.caption: SSO Idle Timeout

auth_protocol.description: The authorization protocol as defined by the caption of auth_protocol_id. In the case of Other, it is defined by the event source.
auth_protocol.caption: Auth Protocol

99.description: The authentication protocol is not mapped. See the auth_protocol attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

auth_protocol_id.description: The normalized identifier of the authentication protocol used by the SSO resource.
auth_protocol_id.caption: Auth Protocol ID

created_time.description: When the SSO resource was created.
created_time.caption: Created Time

duration_mins.description: The duration (in minutes) for an SSO session, after which re-authentication is required.
duration_mins.caption: SSO Session Duration

login_endpoint.description: URL for initiating an SSO login request.
login_endpoint.caption: SSO Login Endpoint

logout_endpoint.description: URL for initiating an SSO logout request, allowing sessions to be terminated across applications.
logout_endpoint.caption: SSO Logout Endpoint

metadata_endpoint.description: URL where metadata about the SSO configuration is available (e.g., for SAML configurations).
metadata_endpoint.caption: SSO Metadata Endpoint

modified_time.description: The most recent time when the SSO resource was updated.
modified_time.caption: Modified Time

scopes.description: Scopes define the specific permissions or actions that the client is allowed to perform on behalf of the user. Each scope represents a different set of permissions, and the user can selectively grant or deny access to specific scopes during the authorization process.
scopes.caption: Scopes

vendor_name.description: Name of the vendor or service provider implementing SSO. E.g., Okta, Auth0, Microsoft.
vendor_name.caption: Service Provider

created_time_dt.description: When the SSO resource was created.
created_time_dt.caption: Created Time

modified_time_dt.description: The most recent time when the SSO resource was updated.
modified_time_dt.caption: Modified Time

sso.name: sso
sso.description: The Single Sign-On (SSO) object provides a structure for normalizing SSO attributes, configuration, and/or settings from Identity Providers.
sso.caption: SSO

org.description:
The Organization object containing details about the organizational unit or management structure that governs the account, subscription, or project where the event or finding was created. This object includes properties such as the organization name, unique identifier, type, and other organizational metadata.

Examples:
  • AWS: Organization object with name, uid (Organization ID), type, and other organizational properties
  • Azure: Management Group object with name, uid (Management Group ID), type, and management group metadata
  • GCP: Organization object with name, uid (Organization ID), type, and organizational attributes
  • Oracle Cloud: Tenancy object with name, uid (Tenancy OCID), type, and tenancy details
org.caption: Organization

account.description:
The Account object containing details about the cloud account, subscription, or billing unit where the event or finding was created. This object includes properties such as the account name, unique identifier, type, labels, and tags.

Examples:
  • AWS: Account object with name, uid (Account ID), type, and other account properties
  • Azure: Subscription object with name, uid (Subscription ID), type, and subscription metadata
  • GCP: Project object with name, uid (Project ID), type, and project attributes
  • Oracle Cloud: Compartment object with name, uid (Tenancy OCID), type, and compartment details
account.caption: Account

cloud_partition.description:
The logical grouping or isolated segment within a cloud provider's infrastructure where the event or finding was created, often used for compliance, governance, or regional separation.

Examples:
  • AWS: Partition where the event occurred (aws, aws-cn, aws-us-gov)
  • Azure: Cloud environment where the event occurred (AzureCloud, AzureUSGovernment, AzureChinaCloud)
cloud_partition.caption: Cloud Partition

project_uid.description: The unique identifier of a Cloud project.
project_uid.caption: Project ID

provider.description: The unique name of the Cloud services provider where the event or finding was created. Examples include AWS, Azure, GCP (Google Cloud Platform), Oracle Cloud, IBM Cloud, Alibaba Cloud, or other public, private, or hybrid cloud providers.
provider.caption: Cloud Provider

region.description:
The cloud region where the event or finding was created, as defined by the cloud provider.

Examples:
  • AWS: Region where the event occurred (us-east-1, eu-west-1)
  • Azure: Region where the event occurred (East US, West Europe)
  • GCP: Region where the event occurred (us-central1, europe-west1)
  • Oracle Cloud: Region where the event occurred (us-ashburn-1, uk-london-1)
region.caption: Region

zone.description:
The availability zone in the cloud region where the event or finding was created, as defined by the cloud provider.

Examples:
  • AWS: Availability zone where the event occurred (us-east-1a, us-east-1b)
  • Azure: Availability zone where the event occurred (1, 2, 3 within a region)
  • GCP: Availability zone where the event occurred (us-central1-a, us-central1-b)
  • Oracle Cloud: Availability zone where the event occurred (AD-1, AD-2, AD-3)
zone.caption: Cloud Availability Zone

cloud.name: cloud
cloud.description: The Cloud object describes the cloud computing environment where an event or finding originated. It provides comprehensive context about the cloud infrastructure, including the cloud service provider, account or subscription details, organizational structure, geographic regions, availability zones, and logical partitions.
cloud.caption: Cloud

name.description: The name or identifier of the message context. In AI systems, this could be the conversation ID, session name, thread identifier, or interaction name (e.g., 'user-session-123', 'conversation-abc', 'chat-thread-456').
name.caption: Name

service.description: The server or service handling the request. In AI systems, this represents the AI service, API endpoint, or agent that processes and responds to requests (e.g., OpenAI API service, Claude API service, internal AI model service).
service.caption: Service

application.description: The initiating client application. In AI systems, this represents the client-side application or framework that initiates requests (e.g., LangChain application, web browser, mobile app, SDK implementation).
application.caption: Application

uid.description: The unique identifier of the message context. This could be a session ID, conversation ID, or other unique identifier that allows correlation of messages within the same context.
uid.caption: Unique ID

3.description: A callable tool or API invoked by the assistant.
3.caption: Tool

6.description: A component that retrieves information or context from a knowledge source (e.g., vector DB, search system, or API).
6.caption: Retriever

1.description: The human user initiating a request.
1.caption: User

2.description: The AI model generating a response.
2.caption: Assistant

99.description: Other role type not covered above.
99.caption: Other

4.description: A software agent that performs tasks with autonomy or delegated intent.
4.caption: Agent

5.description: The component coordinating multiple agents or tools.
5.caption: Orchestrator

ai_role_id.description: Specifies the functional role of the AI within the context of this message, such as retrieving information, assisting reasoning, executing a tool, or generating content.
ai_role_id.caption: AI Role ID

completion_tokens.description: Number of tokens in the model's response/completion for this message.
completion_tokens.caption: Completion Tokens

prompt_tokens.description: Number of tokens in the input prompt for this message.
prompt_tokens.caption: Prompt Tokens

total_tokens.description: Total number of tokens used for this message (prompt + completion).
total_tokens.caption: Total Tokens

message_context.name: message_context
message_context.description: Communication context for AI system interactions including protocols, roles, clients, and session information for MCP and other AI communication systems.

references.description: OWASP AI Security and Privacy Guide - Agent Security

message_context.caption: Message Context

protocol_name.description: The IP protocol name in lowercase, as defined by the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA). For example: tcp or udp.
protocol_name.caption: Protocol Name

protocol_num.description: The IP protocol number, as defined by the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA). For example: 6 for TCP and 17 for UDP.
protocol_num.caption: Protocol Number

port_info.name: port_info
port_info.description: The Port Information object describes a port and its associated protocol details.
port_info.caption: Port Information

name.description: The container name.
name.caption: Name

runtime.description: The backend running the container, such as containerd or cri-o.
runtime.caption: Runtime

size.description: The size of the container image.
size.caption: Size

tag.description: The tag used by the container. It can indicate version, format, OS.
tag.caption: Image Tag

@deprecated.message: Use the labels or tags attribute instead.

uid.caption: Unique ID

image.description: The container image used as a template to run the container.
image.caption: Image

hash.description: Commit hash of image created for docker or the SHA256 hash of the container. For example: 13550340a8681c84c861aac2e5b440161c2b33a3e4f302ac680ca5b686de48de.
hash.caption: Hash

labels.description: The list of labels associated to the container.
labels.caption: Labels

tags.description: The list of tags; {key:value} pairs associated to the container.
tags.caption: Tags

network_driver.description: The network driver used by the container. For example, bridge, overlay, host, none, etc.
network_driver.caption: Network Driver

orchestrator.description: The orchestrator managing the container, such as ECS, EKS, K8s, or OpenShift.
orchestrator.caption: Orchestrator

pod_uuid.description: The unique identifier of the pod (or equivalent) that the container is executing on.
pod_uuid.caption: Pod UUID

container.name: container
container.description: The Container object describes an instance of a specific container. A container is a prepackaged, portable system image that runs isolated on an existing system using a container runtime like containerd.
container.caption: Container

name.description: The Mitigation name that is associated with the attack technique. For example: Password Policies, or Code Signing.
name.caption: Name

uid.description: The Mitigation ID that is associated with the attack technique. For example: M1027, or AML.M0013.
uid.caption: Unique ID

countermeasures.description: The D3FEND countermeasures that are associated with the attack technique. For example: ATT&CK Technique T1003 is addressed by Mitigation M1027, and D3FEND Technique D3-OTP.
countermeasures.caption: Countermeasures

src_url.description: The versioned permalink of the Mitigation. For example: https://attack.mitre.org/versions/v14/mitigations/M1027.
src_url.caption: Source URL

mitigation.name: mitigation
mitigation.description: The MITRE Mitigation object describes the ATT&CK® or ATLAS™ Mitigation ID and/or name that is associated to an attack.

references.description: ATT&CK® Mitigation to D3FEND™ Technique Mappings

mitigation.caption: MITRE Mitigation

name.description: The name of the HTTP header.
name.caption: Name

value.description: The value of the HTTP header.
value.caption: Value

http_header.name: http_header
http_header.description: The HTTP Header object represents the headers sent in an HTTP request or response. HTTP headers are key-value pairs that convey additional information about the HTTP message, including details about the content, caching, authentication, encoding, and other aspects of the communication.
http_header.caption: HTTP Header

name.description: The name of the Identity Provider.
name.caption: Name

state.description: The configuration state of the Identity Provider, normalized to the caption of the state_id value. In the case of Other, it is defined by the event source.

domain.description: The primary domain associated with the Identity Provider.

uid.description: The unique identifier of the Identity Provider.
uid.caption: Unique ID

protocol_name.description: The supported protocol of the Identity Provider. E.g., SAML, OIDC, or OAuth2.
protocol_name.caption: Supported Protocol

issuer.description: The unique identifier (often a URL) used by the Identity Provider as its issuer.

fingerprint.description: The fingerprint of the X.509 certificate used by the Identity Provider.

auth_factors.description: The Authentication Factors object describes the different types of Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) methods and/or devices supported by the Identity Provider.

has_mfa.description: The Identity Provider enforces Multi Factor Authentication (MFA).
has_mfa.caption: MFA Enforced

scim.description: The System for Cross-domain Identity Management (SCIM) resource object provides a structured set of attributes related to SCIM protocols used for identity provisioning and management across cloud-based platforms. It standardizes user and group provisioning details, enabling identity synchronization and lifecycle management with compatible Identity Providers (IdPs) and applications. SCIM is defined in RFC-7634

references.description: System for Cross-domain Identity Management (SCIM) RFC.

scim.caption: SCIM

sso.description: The Single Sign-On (SSO) object provides a structure for normalizing SSO attributes, configuration, and/or settings from Identity Providers.
sso.caption: SSO

tenant_uid.description: The tenant ID associated with the Identity Provider.

url_string.description: The URL for accessing the configuration or metadata of the Identity Provider.

idp.name: idp
idp.description: The Identity Provider object contains detailed information about a provider responsible for creating, maintaining, and managing identity information while offering authentication services to applications. An Identity Provider (IdP) serves as a trusted authority that verifies the identity of users and issues authentication tokens or assertions to enable secure access to applications or services.
idp.caption: Identity Provider

type.description: The type of SBOM, normalized to the caption of the type_id value. In the case of 'Other', it is defined by the source.
type.caption: Type

version.description: The specification (spec) version of the particular SBOM, e.g., 1.6.
version.caption: Version

product.description: Details about the upstream product that generated the SBOM e.g. cdxgen or Syft.
product.caption: Product

uid.description: A unique identifier for the SBOM or the SBOM generation by a source tool, such as the SPDX metadata.component.bom-ref.
uid.caption: SBOM ID

3.description: The International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) publishes, ISO/IEC 19770-2, a standard for software identification (SWID) tags that defines a structured metadata format for describing a software product. A SWID tag document is composed of a structured set of data elements that identify the software product
3.caption: SWID

1.description: System Package Data Exchange (SPDX®) is an open standard capable of representing systems with software components in as SBOMs (Software Bill of Materials) and other AI, data and security references supporting a range of risk management use cases. tags.description: The list of tags; {key:value} pairs associated to the resource.

size.description: The size of the databucket in bytes.

http_request.description: Describes details about the http request associated to the activity that triggered the detection.

name.description: The naming convention or type identifier of the evidence associated with the security detection. For example, the @odata.type from Microsoft Graph Alerts V2 or display_name from CrowdStrike Falcon Incident Behaviors.

user.description: Describes details about the user that was the target or somehow else associated with the activity that triggered the detection.

script.description: Describes details about the script that was associated with the activity that triggered the detection.

uid.description: The unique identifier of the evidence associated with the security detection. For example, the activity_id from CrowdStrike Falcon Alerts or behavior_id from CrowdStrike Falcon Incident Behaviors.

tls.caption: TLS

container.description: Describes details about the container associated to the activity that triggered the detection.
container.caption: Container

database.description: Describes details about the database associated to the activity that triggered the detection.

job.description: Describes details about the scheduled job that was associated with the activity that triggered the detection.

src_endpoint.description: Describes details about the source of the network activity that triggered the detection.

3.description: The verdict for the evidence is that is should be Disregarded.

6.description: The evidence is part of a Test, or other sanctioned behavior(s).

1.description: The verdict for the evidence has been identified as a False Positive.
1.caption: False Positive

2.description: The verdict for the evidence has been identified as a True Positive.
2.caption: True Positive

99.description: The type is not mapped. See the type attribute, which contains a data source specific value.
99.caption: Other

4.caption: Suspicious

5.description: The verdict for the evidence is that the behavior has been identified as Benign.
5.caption: Benign

9.caption: Managed Externally

verdict_id.description: The normalized verdict (or status) ID of the evidence associated with the security detection. For example, Microsoft Graph Security Alerts contain a verdict enumeration for each type of evidence associated with the Alert. This is typically set by an automated investigation process or an analyst/investigator assigned to the finding.
verdict_id.caption: Verdict ID

reg_value.caption: Registry Value

evidences.caption: Evidence Artifacts

size.description: The total size in bytes of all files that were scanned.

type.description: The type of scan.

start_time.description: The timestamp indicating when the scan job began execution.

6.description: The scan was started due to a user logon.

2.caption: Scheduled

99.description: The scan type id is not mapped. See the type attribute, which contains a data source specific value.

7.caption: ELAM

end_time.description: The timestamp indicating when the scan job completed execution.
end_time.caption: End Time

start_time_dt.description: The timestamp indicating when the scan job began execution.
start_time_dt.caption: Start Time

end_time_dt.description: The timestamp indicating when the scan job completed execution.
end_time_dt.caption: End Time

num_files.description: The total number of files analyzed during the scan.
num_files.caption: Scanned Files

num_infected.description: The total number of files identified as infected with malware during the scan.
num_infected.caption: Number of Infected Entities

num_volumes.description: The total number of storage volumes examined during the malware scan.
num_volumes.caption: Number of Volumes

unique_malware_count.description: The number of unique malware detected across all infected files.
unique_malware_count.caption: Unique Malware Count

malware_scan_info.name: malware_scan_info
malware_scan_info.description: The malware scan information object describes characteristics, metadata of a malware scanning job.
malware_scan_info.caption: Malware Scan Info

object.name: object
object.description: An unordered collection of attributes. It defines a set of attributes available in all objects. It can be also used as a generic object to log objects that are not otherwise defined by the schema.
object.caption: Object

modified_time.description: The time when the event was last modified or enriched.
modified_time.caption: Modified Time

logged_time_dt.description:
The time when the logging system collected and logged the event.

This attribute is distinct from the event time in that event time typically contain the time extracted from the original event. Most of the time, these two times will be different.
logged_time_dt.caption: Logged Time

processed_time.description: The event processed time, such as an ETL operation.
processed_time.caption: Processed Time

uid.description: A unique identifier assigned to the OCSF event. This ID is specific to the OCSF event itself and is distinct from the original event identifier in the source system (see original_event_uid).
uid.caption: Event UID

untruncated_size.description: The original size of the OCSF event data in kilobytes before any truncation occurred. This field is typically populated when is_truncated is true to indicate the full size of the original event.
untruncated_size.caption: Untruncated Size

log_format.description: The format of data in the log where the data originated. For example CSV, XML, Windows Multiline, JSON, syslog or Cisco Log Schema.
log_format.caption: Log Source Format

extensions.description: The schema extensions used to create the event.
extensions.caption: Schema Extensions

type.description: The type of the event or finding as a subset of the source of the event. This can be any distinguishing characteristic of the data. For example 'Management Events' or 'Device Penetration Test'.
type.caption: Type

loggers.description: An array of Logger objects that describe the pipeline of devices and logging products between the event source and its eventual destination. Note, this attribute can be used when there is a complex end-to-end path of event flow and/or to track the chain of custody of the data.
loggers.caption: Loggers

log_version.description: The event log schema version of the original event. For example the syslog version or the Cisco Log Schema version
log_version.caption: Log Version

modified_time_dt.description: The time when the event was last modified or enriched.
modified_time_dt.caption: Modified Time

labels.description: The list of labels attached to the event. For example: ["sample", "dev"]
labels.caption: Labels

product.description: The product that reported the event.
product.caption: Product

reporter.description: The entity from which the event or finding was first reported.
reporter.caption: Reporter

sequence.description: Sequence number of the event. The sequence number is a value available in some events, to make the exact ordering of events unambiguous, regardless of the event time precision.
sequence.caption: Sequence Number

logged_time.description:
The time when the logging system collected and logged the event.

This attribute is distinct from the event time in that event time typically contain the time extracted from the original event. Most of the time, these two times will be different.
logged_time.caption: Logged Time

data_classification.description: The Data Classification object includes information about data classification levels and data category types.
data_classification.caption: Data Classification

original_event_uid.description: The unique identifier assigned to the event in its original logging system before transformation to OCSF format. This field preserves the source system's native event identifier, enabling traceability back to the raw log entry. For example, a Windows Event Record ID, a syslog message ID, a Splunk _cd value, or a database transaction log sequence number.
original_event_uid.caption: Original Event ID

transmit_time_dt.description: The time when the event was transmitted from the logging device to it's next destination.
transmit_time_dt.caption: Transmission Time

profiles.description: The list of profiles used to create the event. Profiles should be referenced by their name attribute for core profiles, or extension/name for profiles from extensions.
profiles.caption: Profiles

data_classifications.description: A list of Data Classification objects, that include information about data classification levels and data category types, identified by a classifier.
data_classifications.caption: Data Classification

transformation_info_list.description: An array of transformation info that describes the mappings or transforms applied to the data.
transformation_info_list.caption: Transformation Info

version.description: The version of the OCSF schema, using Semantic Versioning Specification (SemVer). For example: 1.0.0. Event consumers use the version to determine the available event attributes.
version.caption: Version

tags.description: The list of tags; {key:value} pairs associated to the event.
tags.caption: Tags

is_truncated.description: Indicates whether the OCSF event data has been truncated due to size limitations. When true, some event data may have been omitted to fit within system constraints.
is_truncated.caption: Is Truncated

log_source.description: The log system or component where the data originated. For example, a file path, syslog server name or a Windows hostname and logging subsystem such as Security.
log_source.caption: Log Source

source.description: The source of the event or finding. This can be any distinguishing name for the logical origin of the data — for example, 'CloudTrail Events', or a use case like 'Attack Simulations' or 'Vulnerability Scans'.
source.caption: Source

debug.description: Debug information about non-fatal issues with this OCSF event. Each issue is a line in this string array.
debug.caption: Debug Information

processed_time_dt.description: The event processed time, such as an ETL operation.
processed_time_dt.caption: Processed Time

event_code.description: The identifier of the original event. For example the numerical Windows Event Code or Cisco syslog code.
event_code.caption: Event Code

log_level.description: The level at which an event was logged. This can be log provider specific. For example the audit level.
log_level.caption: Log Level

transmit_time.description: The time when the event was transmitted from the logging device to it's next destination.
transmit_time.caption: Transmission Time

correlation_uid.description: A unique identifier used to correlate this OCSF event with other related OCSF events, distinct from the event's uid value. This enables linking multiple OCSF events that are part of the same activity, transaction, or security incident across different systems or time periods.
correlation_uid.caption: Correlation UID

log_provider.description: The logging provider or logging service that logged the event. For example AWS CloudWatch or Splunk.
log_provider.caption: Log Provider

extension.description: The schema extension used to create the event.
extension.caption: Schema Extension

total_queued_duration.description: The amount of time an event spent in a queue awaiting processing. In this case, the value is the difference between processed_time and logged_time. This duration is inclusive of all queues between the originator of the event and the intended long-term storage destination of the event.
total_queued_duration.caption: Total Queued Duration

original_time.description: The original event time as reported by the event source. For example, the time in the original format from system event log such as Syslog on Unix/Linux and the System event file on Windows. Omit if event is generated instead of collected via logs.
original_time.caption: Original Time

log_name.description: The event log name, typically for the consumer of the event. For example, the storage bucket name, SIEM repository index name, etc.
log_name.caption: Log Name

metadata.name: metadata
metadata.description: The Metadata object describes the metadata associated with the event.
metadata.caption: Metadata

name.description: The service or namespace identifier. Examples: s3, ec2 (AWS); Microsoft.Storage (Azure); storage (GCP).
name.caption: Name

all_privileges_unused.description: Indicates whether all privileges within this service are unused.
all_privileges_unused.caption: All Privileges Unused

analyzed_privileges_count.description: The total count of privileges analyzed within this service.
analyzed_privileges_count.caption: Analyzed Privileges Count

execute_count.description: The count of execute-type privileges within this service.
execute_count.caption: Execute Privilege Count

last_used_time.description: The most recent time any privilege in this service was used.
last_used_time.caption: Last Used Time

privilege_attack_info_list.description: The list of privilege-to-attack mappings for this service.
privilege_attack_info_list.caption: Privilege Attack Info List

read_count.description: The count of read-type privileges within this service.
read_count.caption: Read Privilege Count

unused_privileges_count.description: The count of unused privileges within this service.
unused_privileges_count.caption: Unused Privileges Count

write_count.description: The count of write-type privileges within this service.
write_count.caption: Write Privilege Count

last_used_time_dt.description: The most recent time any privilege in this service was used.
last_used_time_dt.caption: Last Used Time

service_privilege_analysis.name: service_privilege_analysis
service_privilege_analysis.description: The Service Privilege Analysis object describes privilege analysis results for a single cloud service or resource namespace. It provides metrics on privilege usage, counts by type, and mappings to potential attack techniques.
service_privilege_analysis.caption: Service Privilege Analysis

name.description: The name of the entity from which the event or finding was reported.
name.caption: Name

ip.description: The IP address of the entity from which the event or finding was reported.
ip.caption: IP Address

hostname.description: The hostname of the entity from which the event or finding was reported.
hostname.caption: Hostname

uid.description: The unique identifier of the entity from which the event or finding was reported.
uid.caption: Unique ID

org.description: The organization properties of the entity that reported the event or finding.
org.caption: Organization

reporter.name: reporter
reporter.description: The entity from which an event or finding was reported.
reporter.caption: Reporter

name.description: The name of the classifier.
name.caption: Name

type.description: The type of the classifier.
type.caption: Type

uid.description: The unique identifier of the classifier.
uid.caption: Unique ID

classifier_details.name: classifier_details
classifier_details.description: The Classifier Details object describes details about the classifier used for data classification.
classifier_details.caption: Classifier Details

observation_parameter.description: The specific parameter, metric or property where the anomaly was observed. Examples include: CPU usage percentage, API response time in milliseconds, HTTP error rate, memory utilization, network latency, transaction volume, etc. This helps identify the exact aspect of the system exhibiting anomalous behavior.
observation_parameter.caption: Observation Parameter

observation_type.description: The type of analysis methodology used to detect the anomaly. This indicates how the anomaly was identified through different analytical approaches. Common types include: Frequency Analysis, Time Pattern Analysis, Volume Analysis, Sequence Analysis, Distribution Analysis, etc.
observation_type.caption: Observation Type

observations.description: Details about the observed anomaly or observations that were flagged as anomalous compared to expected baseline behavior.
observations.caption: Observations

observed_pattern.description: The specific pattern identified within the observation type. For Frequency Analysis, this could be 'FREQUENT', 'INFREQUENT', 'RARE', or 'UNSEEN'. For Time Pattern Analysis, this could be 'BUSINESS_HOURS', 'OFF_HOURS', or 'UNUSUAL_TIME'. For Volume Analysis, this could be 'NORMAL_VOLUME', 'HIGH_VOLUME', or 'SURGE'. The pattern values are specific to each observation type and indicate how the observed behavior relates to the baseline.
observed_pattern.caption: Observed Pattern

anomaly.name: anomaly
anomaly.description: Describes an anomaly or deviation detected in a system. Anomalies are unexpected activity patterns that could indicate potential issues needing attention.
anomaly.caption: Anomaly

name.description: The human-friendly name of a token or key, if available, such as the name from the Okta API Token API.
name.caption: Name
